era_context: The Middle Bronze Age (MBA) in the Levant and Egypt; the Second Intermediate Period in Egypt (13th–17th dynasties fragmented); Babylon under Hammurabi's successors experiencing dynastic decline; the Hittite Old Kingdom consolidating power in Anatolia; Mycenaean Greece in the Early Bronze Age III period with palace complexes developing; the Shang dynasty in China in its early phases with oracle bone divination practices established.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited archaeological dating precision for this period)
Southeast Asia (sparse written records; archaeological sequences not yet firmly dated to specific years)
Mesoamerica (no written chronology; radiocarbon dating has wide margins of error)

events.title: Reign of Samsu-iluna of Babylon (approximate period)
events.description: Samsu-iluna, son and successor of Hammurabi, ruled during a period of declining Babylonian power. His reign saw the loss of territorial control in the south to the Sealand Dynasty and pressure from Hittite expansion in the north. The empire contracted significantly from Hammurabi's zenith. Samsu-iluna's inscriptions and administrative documents attest to military campaigns and internal consolidation efforts, though with diminishing success.

sources.name: Babylonian King List (later compilation, but based on contemporary records)

events.certainty_note: Samsu-iluna's reign is dated to approximately 1749–1712 BCE by most Mesopotamian chronologies (Middle Chronology). The specific year -1780 falls within this range but cannot be pinpointed to a single year without additional synchronisms. Dating relies on king list lengths and astronomical calculations.

events.title: Hittite consolidation under Hantili I (approximate period)
events.description: Hantili I, founder of the Hittite Old Kingdom, consolidated power in central Anatolia after the collapse of the Amorite dynasty. He established the capital at Hattusa and began the process of centralizing Hittite authority. His reign marked the transition from the Old Assyrian trading network dominance to Hittite political ascendancy in the region. Hittite cuneiform texts and administrative records document his military campaigns and administrative reforms.

sources.name: Hittite King List and royal inscriptions

events.certainty_note: Hantili I's reign is traditionally dated to c. 1650–1620 BCE (Middle Chronology), placing -1780 outside his confirmed reign. However, the broader consolidation period of Hittite power in the early Old Kingdom spans this era. Chronological uncertainty is substantial for this period.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Samsu-iluna's reign is subject to significant chronological debate. The 'Middle Chronology' places him c. 1749–1712 BCE, but the 'Low Chronology' dates him to c. 1697–1660 BCE, and the 'High Chronology' to c. 1813–1791 BCE. The year -1780 may or may not fall within his reign depending on which chronological system is adopted. Similarly, Hittite chronology for this period is uncertain, with some scholars proposing alternative dates for Hantili I's reign. No specific events can be confidently assigned to the year -1780 with high certainty.
historiographic_note: The historical record for -1780 is extremely sparse and dependent on king lists, which are themselves problematic sources (they often compress or extend reigns, and synchronisms with Egyptian chronology are disputed). Contemporary written records exist from Mesopotamia and Anatolia, but they are not dated to specific years in the modern sense. Chronological reconstruction relies on astronomical calculations (Venus observations in the Ammisaduqa period), radiocarbon dating, and synchronisms with Egyptian dynasties—all of which have margins of error of ±50–100 years or more. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward political and military narratives from royal courts; economic, social, and cultural life is poorly documented. Archaeological evidence is fragmentary and often difficult to date precisely.

graph_edges.note: Samsu-iluna's reign represents the beginning of Babylon's territorial contraction, a process that accelerated over the following decades.
